cards.text: {{c1::Multiple inheritance}} enables a derived class to inherit members from {{c2::more than one}} parent.

To use multiple inheritance, specify each base class separated by a {{c1::comma}}:
class Teacher : public {{c2::Person, public Employee}}

cards.front: What is a mixin in C++?
cards.back: A small class that can be inherited from to add properties to a class. It's intended to be mixed into other classes, not instantiated on its own.

cards.question: What's the problem with this code?
cards.explanation: WirelessAdapter has TWO getID() functions (one from USBDevice, one from NetworkDevice), so c54G.getID() is ambiguous. Must use c54G.USBDevice::getID() or c54G.NetworkDevice::getID().

cards.front: What is the "diamond problem" (or "diamond of doom") in multiple inheritance?
cards.back: When a class multiply inherits from two classes which each inherit from a single base class, creating a diamond-shaped inheritance pattern. This raises issues about whether the derived class has one or two copies of the base.

cards.text: {{c1::Avoid}} multiple inheritance unless alternatives lead to more {{c2::complexity}}.

cards.front: Why do many modern languages (Java, C#) restrict or disallow multiple inheritance?
cards.back: Because it makes the language too complex and ultimately causes more problems than it fixes. These languages often allow multiple inheritance of interface classes only.

cards.explanation:
Mixins are small classes designed to add specific functionality when inherited:
  • Not meant to be instantiated alone
  • Mixed into other classes via multiple inheritance
  • Often use scope resolution to avoid ambiguity
  • Typically don't use virtual functions
  • Often implemented as templates
examples: class Button : public Box, public Label, public Tooltip { };

cards.front: What is CRTP (Curiously Recurring Template Pattern)?
cards.back: When a derived class inherits from a mixin base class using the derived class itself as a template type parameter: class Derived : public Mixin<Derived> { };

cards.front: How do you resolve an ambiguous function call in multiple inheritance?
cards.back: Use explicit scope resolution to specify which parent class's version you want: object.ParentClass::functionName()

cards.front: Can most problems solved with multiple inheritance also be solved with single inheritance?
cards.back: Yes, most problems can be solved using single inheritance instead. Multiple inheritance should only be used when alternatives would lead to more complexity.

cards.front: Name two C++ standard library objects that use multiple inheritance.
cards.back: std::cin and std::cout are both implemented using multiple inheritance.
